query: What is the doctrine of equivalents in patent infringement?
ground_truth_contexts: The doctrine of equivalents allows a patent holder to claim infringement even if the accused product does not literally fall within the patent claims, provided the differences are insubstantial.
ground_truth_answer: The doctrine of equivalents allows patent holders to claim infringement when an accused product performs substantially the same function in substantially the same way to achieve substantially the same result as the claimed invention, even if there is no literal infringement.

query: What triggers the on-sale bar under 35 U.S.C. 102?
ground_truth_contexts: Under 35 U.S.C. 102(b)(1), a patent is barred if the claimed invention was on sale or in public use more than one year before the effective filing date of the patent application.
ground_truth_answer: The on-sale bar is triggered when the claimed invention is the subject of a commercial offer for sale and is ready for patenting, occurring more than one year before the effective filing date of the patent application.
